segments.title: Why historians seek out physical history
segments.summary: Historians visit places and objects because seeing and touching material evidence creates a different understanding from reading about it.
segments.body: Historians visit places and surviving objects because physical encounter produces a different understanding from reading alone. Dr. Clarke names the Ship Shape crew's trips to Japan and Scandinavia as examples of this practice. Seeing and touching material evidence makes the past tangible in a way that a written description cannot fully reproduce. The method supplements documentary research with impressions available only in person.
segments.question: Why do historians like to visit historical places and objects in person?
segments.answerShort: Because seeing, touching, and standing beside physical evidence provides an understanding that reading alone cannot supply.

evidence.note: He contrasts physical encounter with reading and says it produces a different understanding.

segments.title: A surviving HMS Warspite armour fragment
segments.summary: A curved, heavy fragment of HMS Warspite barbette steel makes the scale and material presence of a battleship immediately visible.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses a surviving piece of HMS Warspite's barbette steel as a compact demonstration of material history. Feeling its curvature, thickness, and weight makes the scale of a battleship's protected gun position more immediate than a specification or photograph can. He emphasizes that this is a rare surviving fragment, displayed unexpectedly in a local car park rather than a formal museum. Its physical presence gives an object from a famous warship an emotional as well as technical force. The example shows how naval heritage can preserve evidence of construction and scale even when most of the ship itself no longer survives.

evidence.note: He identifies the fragment as HMS Warspite barbette steel and discusses its curvature, thickness, weight, and rarity.
He describes the fragment's emotional force, its unusual car-park setting, and why physical history matters especially for naval historians.
